title: In-process jit dispatch cannot execute tools — executeAgent is a single-turn completion primitive, so the grant machinery clamps a capability that does not exist
canonical_vocabulary: executable tool binding in jit dispatch
description: executeAgent (packages/pi-jit-agents/src/jit-runtime.ts:432-738) makes one completeFn call and extracts output; it never binds tool implementations. compiled.tools is consumed at exactly one point — the DEC-0047 grant-subset clamp (computeGrantViolation, :488) — gating NAMES, materializing nothing; the only tool ever passed to the LLM is the phantom output-schema tool (:566-570), never executed by design; DispatchContext (types.ts:195-234) has no channel for tool implementations. Empirically demonstrated (instrumented completeFn against built dist with the WO-001 spec): grant ['write','bash'] passes the clamp and is discarded — toolsPassedToLLM: UNDEFINED. The primitive fits its original consumers (monitor classifiers, structured-output workflow steps — output, never action); the work-order loop is the first consumer dispatching an agent whose job is to ACT, and reused the act-less primitive. Class: every in-process jit dispatch (call-agent, run-work-order-loop, future consumers reading .agent.yaml tools: as capability); real tool execution exists only in a pi subprocess. Structural sibling: FGAP-008 (closed — a contract lacking the channel its consumers assume). Severity within the surface: CRITICAL — every honest work-order must fail; the surface was unexercised (zero live WOs) at filing.

evidence.reference: 432-738 executeAgent: single completeFn call (:572); context.tools populated only by the phantom output-schema tool (:566-570); grant clamp consumes compiled.tools at :488 and nothing else does
195-234 DispatchContext — no tool-implementation channel; 188-189 CompiledAgent.tools documented as grant vocabulary
Break 2: instrumented reproduction (SPEC tools ['write','bash'] → toolsPassedToLLM UNDEFINED), class inventory, why-it-is-shaped-this-way archaeology

impact: The work-order loop's core promise is void: a dispatched target agent cannot produce any side-effect, so iteration 0 can never pass a real check requiring one — every real work-order fails or aborts. call-agent consumers inherit the same ceiling. The deterministic real-check surfaced this as a clean failure rather than a false pass.
proposed_resolution: Decided under FEAT-014's dispatch-architecture decision: (a) dispatch acting agents as pi subprocesses (tools exist there; executeAgent stays the classify/structured-output primitive), (b) grow executeAgent a bound tool-execution loop, or (c) re-scope work-orders to output-only. Whichever is decided, the grant machinery must clamp a capability that actually exists on the dispatch path, and the WO-001 probe recipe becomes the regression pin.
